segments.text: Our third candidate is Rafael Grossi, a name you may recognize because he's in the news.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: He's an Argentine diplomat with a long career, and he has been leading the International Atomic Energy Agency, the IAEA, since 2010.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: He's the first person from Latin America to do so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: He was re-elected.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: It's a hard thing to do to take on a second term.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: And Rafael Grossi is nominated by his own country, Argentina.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Please join me in welcoming Rafael Grossi.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Good to see you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I don't know how he has time to run for Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I mean, he's trying to find a way out of the Iran war.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: But anyway, that's his commitment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: That is the case.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Welcome, Rafael Grossi.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We're going to give you five minutes to set out your vision about how you see the job as UN Secretary General and how you're going to do it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Five minutes and I will give you a four minute warning.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Very good.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, thank you, thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's great to be here, this historic place, and to see that so many are equally concerned and supportive of the United Nations and a multilateral answer to the problems of the day.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This next election,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: of the Secretary General of the United Nations, in my opinion, might arguably be the most consequential one in the history of the organization.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And I believe this because
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: of the diagnostic on which I think everybody would more or less agree on how we see the world conflict, fragmentation, polarization, the return of interstate war.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: global crisis of different natures.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But what is especially worrying this time and for this particular selection process is that we see a fundamental, I would say, crisis of confidence in the United Nations, in the possibility and in the ability of the United Nations to be a factor that would be able to influence
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: precisely world affairs and in particular the pursuit of international peace and security.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I said just now interstate war has returned after many years here in Europe but also in Africa and in many other places and one dismaying
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: element that we see when we look at each and every crisis, you know, from Africa in Sudan, of course, to Ukraine and Russia to the Middle East or Cambodia and Thailand or India and Pakistan or Azerbaijan and Armenia in its time, is one constatation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The UN is not there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: the UN is absent from the managing or the resolution of any of the conflicts I have just mentioned.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And it needn't be so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It needn't be so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Some say the United Nations has to play a role in this thing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, it's not going to happen unless we do things differently.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's not going to happen because we believe in that or there's a principle about that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It is only going to happen when there is a conviction in leaders in belligerent nations that the participation of the United Nations, and in particular the Secretary General, is going to
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: facilitate a better outcome than what they are currently having.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And this conviction grew in me because of my own experience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's not an idea that I had or some words that I tried to put nicely on a piece of paper in the vision.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This is what I have been doing in Russia and Ukraine, under fire,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: a front line, a separation of the power plant between China and Japan, Iran and Israel and the United States.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In many places, on many occasions, I have seen firsthand and through my own experience that it is possible to play a role, and not only to play a role for the sake of playing a role,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: is that you can and the multilateral platform, in this case the IEA, but it embodies in a certain sense the logic and the spirit of the United Nations, we can see that this or through this intervention we can prevent a nuclear accident
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: we can provide platforms for a solution on the ongoing crisis about Iran or before that Syria or before that Iraq and all of these situations that we've had.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So this idea that there is a centrality of the peace and security mission is not because there is a dereliction or any form of marginalization of the other pillars of the UN mission.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It is because we have to look at what is happening in the world right now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And when war reigns, there is no human right to be defended.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: When war reigns, there is no developmental mission that can remotely be successful.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: That is very, very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Of course, when it comes to development, this requires a profound soul search when it comes to the work of the United Nations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: By the metrics,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: that the UN applied to itself, which would be what?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Sustainable development goals.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: When we look at that, and mind you, 2030 is around the corner.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's a rules-based order in your five minutes are up.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: You called it a consequential decision.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's been said many times tonight.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Everyone here agrees with that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: We also heard from the former Prime Minister of New Zealand, former UN official, Helen Clark, who said it is so consequential that now is the time for a woman to lead the organization.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Why are you the best woman for the job?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: When we, I think this has to be taken very seriously.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What, the need for a woman or?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: The issue of man or woman.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: the issue of a man or a woman.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: When we are selecting Secretary General, we are selecting a person, the best person for the job, the best person for the job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And to be the best person, the best person to incarnate this crucial function,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: There is track record, there is merit, there is the capacity to federate, there is the capacity to generate consensus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: it can be a man, it can be a woman.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And I say this from the moral comfort of being myself a gender champion, from having taken a technical organization that when I took it over had 28% of women professionals and today it has 53% in the space of five years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I don't need anybody to tell me about
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: this so we need to select the best and we have seen tonight we are having the privilege to listen to very distinguished women candidates so the sacrosanct principle here is the equality of possibilities and opportunities between men and women as simple as that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Can you briefly give us a sense of the kind of skills and experience you would bring to the job?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: For example, in your role as the head of IAA, you have really had a difficult juggling act between members of the UN, in this case over Iran's nuclear program, of course an escalating war.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: What have you learned in this process that makes you the person for this job?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Well, I think that I was trying to address this just now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I have seen in this job, which is still ongoing, we see what is happening with Iran and the negotiations, that it is possible to be an impartial but active and influential
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: player.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It has been possible for me to talk to Vladimir Putin several times and then to go to Volodymyr Zelensky several times and then continue and to iterate until I find a solution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So it's not a theory for me.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's my job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's what I do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Only thing that the UN would allow us to be operative in a wider platform.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We'd like some commitments from you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: This is on the record.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: People are taking notes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: The one for 8 billion campaign and the UN Associated UK is a founding partner in this, has called on all candidates to commit publicly to five principles during this campaign.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: One, publishing financial disclosures.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Two, engaging with civil society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Three, participating in transparent general assembly and security council processes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Four, respecting
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Article 100 of the UN Charter, which as you know means independence for Member States, and avoiding conflicts of interest with any current UN rules.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Do you commit to those five principles, and if so, why?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think in those principles there is so much common sense that it would be hard to imagine that any candidate or anybody would have a problem with those.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Subscribing formally, I can't.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I am an elected, I remember, I am an elected leader of an organization and so the only principles I can subscribe to are those that are produced by my policy-making organs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But of course, those principles are sane, are full of, I would say, common sense.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So who can have a problem with that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Let's start with questions from the audience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: This one comes from Georgios Costakos.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: He's with the Peace Reflection Group of former UN staff, all the way from Greece.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Now his question, you've touched on this already.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: How will you publicly speak truth to power and invoke your political authority under Article 99 of the UN Charter to bring even major power violations before the Security Council?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: upholding the peaceful settlement of disputes and pursuing active, quiet diplomacy and mediation as appropriate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: There are two elements there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: There is one specific in the Charter, which is Article 99, when the Secretary-General feels there is one issue.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And then there is the array of issues that he mentions in his question, which come under Chapter 6 of the Charter, when you have all the means of diplomatic expression that a secretary-general can bring to the job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: doing all these things in consulting, in talking, in proposing, which is what we have been doing at the IAEA.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I believe in a Secretary General there is constantly engaging in this kind of activity as opposed to
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Article 99 as seeing as something that you only touch or you press the button and then something magic is going to happen.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It has been invoked and nothing happened.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I believe in a constant, of course, that is a competence and the drafters of the charter were wise in indicating that the secretary general is not just a chief administrative officer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's much more than that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So just very briefly, to be clear, you believe that you should use your megaphone, you should speak out, you should in some ways name in shame?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Well, it depends.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It depends.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Since we are in a church,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: You know, remember the gospel, there is time to talk.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: There is a time not to do it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: There is a time to observe.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: There is a time to laugh, and there is a time to cry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And pray.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Absolutely.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I think, of course, the secretary general is a voice, much more than a voice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And on occasion, he or she may need to express principles.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But for me, principles must be expressed in a pragmatic way.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Because the Secretary General is not a force which is there as a moral compass of the world or a judge that is attributing guilty or innocence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He or she must be an active actor in international relations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And this is what we so desperately need at this moment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: A question from India, from Mandeep Tiwana from Civicus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: The present critique of the UN is that it's far too state-centric.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: What are your plans to make the UN truly a people's United Nations that enables meaningful participation of citizens and civil society in resolving the world's problems?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Yeah, indeed.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Of course, the UN is an intergovernmental organization and member states are the ones taking decisions, and that is the normal setup of the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: At the same time, we live in a world where the expression, societal expression, has a role.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And it has been having that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Your plans, though, to make this happen?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: It is happening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It is.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I believe it is happening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It should.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, if you mean by plan having an office or creating more bureaucracy for that, no, because it's not needed.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Of course, you talk to everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: You have to be at the end of the day, we are accountable to the people out there who, by the way, have a very poor opinion of the UN, if we care to ask.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: A question from the floor now, from Sanam Nargi Anderlini from the International Civil Society Action Network.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Hello.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: The Women, Peace and Security agenda passed in 2000 with 10 Security Council resolutions has been one of the most transformative agendas addressing conflict prevention, resolution, terrorism, security and humanitarian issues.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: but it's been siloed and implemented in ad hoc ways, disjointed from the women-led civil society ecosystem that is leading much of the crisis response around the world globally that we have, and it's never had senior leadership at the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Civil society has long called for a special representative of the Secretary General,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: on women peace and security to enable the effective action and the efficiency needed.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: What plans do you have to elevate this agenda, partner with women's peace building networks, and to realize the potential of the agenda and the promise of we the people of the United Nations?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Of course, those words have resonated ever since 1945, we the peoples.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But as I said before, I am a bit skeptical about creating offices, creating representatives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In fact, one of my ideas is to reduce the number of special representatives and special offices.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I think the Secretary General has a political responsibility to do some of the things that you are saying and being accountable for those.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Even if you don't appoint a special envoy, do you agree with Sanam that more work has to be done on the women, peace and security?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: It's an evidence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's an evidence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's an evidence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But this is why I say that it is in the area of international peace and security there is a moral and statutory obligation for the Secretary General to assume these roles.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This proliferation of figures has become too much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: and it has to be stopped.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I can tell you that if you get the job, people like Sanam will make...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Absolutely, she will be more than welcome.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Question from the audience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Peter Reavy is here tonight.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Where are you?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Peter's not here?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Not there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Any other Peters?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I'll read Peter's question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: With the recent successful UN resolution for all UN member states to take all possible steps to avoid causing significant damage to the climate, what will you do to ensure the world we all share together will be at the heart of their work?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: So, climate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Well, one of the biggest challenges we have collectively, I would say, and one which is
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I would say closely intertwined with the discussions on energy, on energy security.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: These are agendas that have come combined in the past few years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I think we have reached important milestones in terms of climate, in terms of what we need to do, and at the same time we see that we are far from that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: A little bit
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What I said in development, we see that there is a divergence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Some things have been done, the International Panel on Climate Change, and these are things that we follow very closely from the IAEA, because we are the only organization in the system that has energy in the title.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So we are following that, but we see that there is a discrepancy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: between certain goals that are diplomatically agreed and the possibility to bring them forward.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I think there has to be a more synchronic work in terms of energy and the climate diplomacy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It is also very, very important, I think, after 30 conferences, and I think we have endured a few of those, a conference of the parties, these COPs have become an unmanageable
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I don't know, I suppose that some of you, maybe several, have been attending those and now it has become a yearly manifestation with thousands of organizations, commercial sites, so I think there's a need to refocus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: because in particular, vulnerable countries, small island development states, et cetera, they do have an existential stake on issues related to climate, but also other countries have an economic stake on what they need for their economic development in terms of energy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So trying to bring together those agendas, for me, it will be very, very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: and maybe even an end to those COP conferences.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: I think Hannah Musavi from the lovingly named Pomegranate House, gender and governance NGO.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Hello.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Hi, thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So given the rapidly changing global environment, including the shift in funding streams and the growing influence of major donors, how will you safeguard the integrity and the independence of the United Nations so it continues to serve all nations rather than just the interests of the most powerful actors
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: or contributors, and this also relates to conflicting agendas that these actors may have versus the United Nations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: An important issue, and we've been having important conversations with philanthropists today.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This is very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: For one thing, I think we need to
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: make it so that we re-engage with the membership and that assessed contributions are paid.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Without that, it will be very difficult for us, for the organization, to carry out its mission.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: As the Secretary General has rightly described, we are facing a very, very serious financial crisis.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: at the moment, which compromises the ability of the UN to work or to work efficiently.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: For that to be corrected, I think there is this trust deficit that I was pointing out in my introductory words, in the sense that there has to be a reconnection
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: between the Secretary General and the major contributing countries to make sure that we can have again, because this has been broken, we have again a constructive relationship when countries are willingly and convinced of the support they're giving us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In terms of donors, when you were referring to that, you were referring to private sector or non-traditional donors, if I may ask you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In general.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah, in general.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, I addressed the state side of the equation, which has to do with this deficit in terms of trust, confidence that needs to be repaired.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In terms of others, I think the UN, as you know, has
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: in place important guardrails or norms to ensure that out of voluntary contributions coming from the private sector, no undue influence comes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But I think it is very important to be able to, I would say, engage even more
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And I was referring to this conversation I was having with philanthropists like the other candidates today.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Look at this example.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In today's world, the Gates Foundation has become the second largest contributor to the World Health Organization.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02
